name: Ansible Python API (Automation Framework)
pricing_notes: Open source (GPL-3.0); Red Hat; AWX/Tower for enterprise UI

auth.notes: SSH keys or passwords for managed nodes; become (sudo) for privilege escalation

performance.notes: SSH-based; parallel forks for multiple hosts; agentless

agent_readiness.notes: Agentless IT automation via SSH and YAML playbooks. Python API (internal, semi-stable): from ansible.executor.playbook_executor import PlaybookExecutor. ansible-runner for stable Python API. CLI: ansible-playbook site.yml -i inventory.ini. Playbooks: tasks using modules (apt, yum, copy, template, service, systemd). Idempotent by design. 5000+ community collections on Ansible Galaxy. AWX/Tower for web UI and scheduling. Configuration management, application deployment, cloud provisioning.

gotchas: Python API is internal and unstable — use ansible-runner for Python integration
SSH-based execution is slow for large fleets vs agent-based tools
YAML playbooks can become complex and hard to maintain at scale
best_when: Agentless configuration management across Linux servers
Application deployment playbooks with idempotent modules
Teams preferring YAML DSL for infrastructure automation
avoid_when: When Python API directly needed (use ansible-runner instead)
Very large fleets (Salt or Puppet agent-based faster)
summary: Ansible automates IT via SSH and YAML playbooks — agentless configuration management. Use ansible-runner for Python API integration. 5000+ collections on Galaxy. Idempotent modules: apt, copy, template, service, systemd, cloud provisioning. AWX/Tower for enterprise UI. GPL-3.0. Slower than agent-based tools for large fleets but zero-agent deployment makes adoption easy.
